# crimes

> crimes is an open-source command-line tool that scans TypeScript and
> JavaScript repositories for change risk and AI-agent risk. It runs
> locally with no cloud calls, ships a stable versioned JSON contract
> so AI coding agents can consume its output safely, and is MIT-licensed.

crimes is positioned narrowly. It is not a linter (ESLint, Biome),
not a security scanner (Semgrep, CodeQL), and not a code-quality
platform (SonarQube). Its wedge is local, open-source, agent-native
codebase risk and context — the question "where is future change most
likely to go wrong, and what should a human or AI agent know before
editing it?".

Core principles:
- Deterministic before magical. Detectors must work without any LLM.
- Evidence before judgement. Every finding cites concrete AST or git
  evidence (line ranges, churn counts, import patterns).
- JSON output is the product contract. Every report carries a
  `schema_version` and a `report_type` discriminator
  (`"scan"`, `"context"`, `"hotspots"`, `"diff"`, `"baseline"`,
  `"baseline_check"`, `"verdict"`). Breaking changes bump
  `schema_version`.
- Built for AI agents. Ships a Claude Code skill and AGENTS.md so any
  agent (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Aider, Continue, Copilot
  Workspace) can use it without per-prompt setup.

## Install

- npm: `npm install -g crimes`
- one-shot: `npx crimes scan .`
- requires Node.js 18+

## Commands (latest published v0.9.2)

`crimes feedback` (shipped in v0.7.0) — capture per-finding verdicts that
feed the calibration loop:
- `crimes feedback <fingerprint> --verdict {tp|fp|known} --note '...'`
  — write a verdict. `fp` requires `--note` and writes a
  feedback-sourced suppression pinned to the current crimes minor.
- `crimes feedback list [--global] [--since 30d] [--verdict tp|fp|known]`
- `crimes feedback summary [--global]` — by_verdict / by_detector /
  by_version / by_repo (only with --global) aggregates.
- `crimes feedback export [--format jsonl|md|json] [--append-global]`
  — append-global pushes local entries to
  `~/.crimes/feedback-rollup.jsonl`, deduped by
  `(repo, timestamp, fingerprint)`.
- `crimes feedback recheck [--detector <type>]` — per-release review
  surface; walks every feedback-sourced suppression whose pinned
  minor is older than the current one, prints the re-feedback
  commands per resurfaced finding.



Per-file / per-directory (shipped in v0.1.0):
- `crimes scan .` — scan repo, print top findings
- `crimes scan . --format json` — emit the versioned JSON contract
- `crimes scan --changed --format json` — working-tree-only post-edit scan
- `crimes context <file> --format json` — pre-edit briefing for one file
- `crimes hotspots --since 90d --format json` — churn × findings ranking

Branch / PR safety (shipped in v0.2.0):
- `crimes scan --changed --fail-on low|medium|high` — exit 1 when the
  changed set has a finding at or above the threshold. Only valid with
  `--changed`. JSON gains `fail_on` and `failed` fields.
- `crimes diff <base...head> --format json` — new / fixed / unchanged
  crimes between two committed refs (triple-dot range). Working-tree-safe;
  exports each ref via `git archive` into a temp dir. Findings matched
  by stable fingerprint `<type>::<file>::<symbol-or-empty>`.
- `crimes baseline save` — snapshot current findings to
  `.crimes/baseline.json` (intended to be committed).
- `crimes baseline check --fail-on low|medium|high` — re-scan and fail
  only on findings absent from the baseline. Default threshold `medium`.
- `crimes verdict --base origin/main --format json` — one-line "did
  this branch make the repo cleaner, worse, unchanged, or mixed?"
  Advisory by default; opt into a hard gate with `--fail-on worse |
  new-high | new-medium`.

## CI integration (v0.2.0)

Three deterministic gating modes — all share a uniform exit-code
contract: `0` success, `1` configured `--fail-on` threshold met,
`2` usage / environment error.

- Mode A — `crimes scan --changed --fail-on high` (narrow,
  changed-files-only).
- Mode B — `crimes baseline check --fail-on medium` (legacy repo;
  pin debt with a committed `.crimes/baseline.json`).
- Mode C — `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
  (PR summary that flips to a hard gate on any new high finding).

Copy-paste GitHub Actions workflow ships in the repo at
`examples/github-actions/crimes.yml`. Full integration guide:
`docs/ci.md`.

## Structural detectors shipped today (v0.1.0)

- `large_function` (charge: God Function) — functions over a body-line threshold
- `large_file` (charge: God File) — files over a line threshold
- `todo_density` (charge: Unfinished Business) — clusters of TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK
- `direct_date` (charge: Temporal Recklessness) — `Date.now()` / `new Date()`
  used directly in domain code

## Information architecture detectors (new in v0.3.0)

These five IA detectors are new in v0.3.0. They are deterministic:
**no LLM, no API key, and no network access** is required. Every
finding cites concrete files, line numbers, and string literals;
cross-file findings populate `related_files` and render as an "Also
touches:" block in the human report.

- `missing_agent_context` (charge: Missing Agent Context) — repos that
  declare a `bin` in `package.json` but ship no `AGENTS.md`,
  `CLAUDE.md`, or `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`.
- `route_metadata_drift` (charge: Route Metadata Drift) — route path,
  file location, default-export component name, `<title>`,
  `metadata.title`, and nav-source labels disagree for the same
  destination (≥3-source quorum).
- `duplicated_navigation_source` (charge: Duplicated Navigation Source)
  — a single internal destination appears in two or more nav-like
  sources with different non-empty labels.
- `concept_alias_drift` (charge: Concept Alias Drift) — multiple
  aliases from a seeded concept group (`team` / `workspace` / `org`;
  `plan` / `subscription` / `tier`; etc.) appear across the product
  surface, each in ≥2 distinct directories. Capped at the three
  strongest groups per scan.
- `docs_code_drift` (charge: Docs-Code Drift) — a markdown doc under
  `docs/` (or a root-level `*.md`) contains a local link that does not
  resolve on disk.

IA findings phrase summaries as "appears to" / "may" — they are
**ambiguity signals**, not claims of semantic truth. The
interpretation belongs to the human or agent reading the report. Full
reference (quorum rules, false-positive notes, suggested fixes):
`docs/finding-types/ia.md`.

Every finding (structural and IA) includes typed scores: `severity`,
`confidence`, and `agent_risk`. Default ranking is by aggregate risk,
so multiple sources of truth, weak tests, and hidden side effects can
elevate a finding even when local severity is low.

## Agent integration

- Claude Code: `.claude/skills/crimes/SKILL.md` auto-loads in any repo.
- Other agents: a top-level `AGENTS.md` is read by OpenAI Codex CLI,
  Cursor, Aider, Continue, and GitHub Copilot Workspace.

Recommended loop for any agent:
1. Before editing a file:   `crimes context <file> --format json`
2. After editing:           `crimes scan --changed --format json`
3. Before opening the PR:   `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
4. Triaging the repo:       `crimes hotspots --format json`
5. Any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by the edit is a blocker.

## Shipped in v0.3.0 — information architecture crimes

v0.2.0 made crimes useful for branches, PRs, CI, and agent loops.
v0.3.0 focused on detecting repo structure drift that confuses
humans, coding agents, teams, and customers — source-of-truth and
concept drift across code, routes, navigation, copy, docs, and
ownership. The five IA detectors listed above are the headline slice.
Live on npm.

## Shipped in v0.4.0 — agent context quality and signal-to-noise

v0.4.0 ships no new detectors. It raises the trust ratio of every
detector that already shipped. Headline additions:

- `ContextReport.related_files` — up to ten files an agent should
  read before editing the target, ranked by a deterministic blend
  of IA-finding passthrough, shared path tokens, domain-prefix
  filename matches, and same-directory siblings. Each entry carries
  a `reason` and an ordinal `score`.
- Monorepo / nested-package root auto-detection for `crimes context`
  — walks up to the nearest enclosing `package.json`; explicit
  `--root` still wins.
- Shape-aware `large_function` — per-shape thresholds for `domain`,
  `test_callback`, `react_component`, `page_export`, `route_handler`,
  and `unknown` functions. React pages, route handlers, and test
  callbacks no longer trip the detector at the 60-line domain
  threshold.
- `docs_code_drift` GitHub-relative link allowlist — `../../issues`,
  `../../pull/N`, `../../wiki/Home`, `../../blob/...` and similar
  GitHub-rewritten paths no longer flag as broken local links.
- `ScanReport.changed_files` — `crimes scan --changed --format json`
  emits a top-level array of every file the resolver returned,
  sorted and deduplicated, including files with zero findings.
- `HotspotsReport.history_limited` (+ `history_limited_reason`) —
  set when `git rev-parse --is-shallow-repository` returns true.
  Agents should downweight rankings when the flag is set.
- Top-level `agent_guidance` ordering in `ContextReport` — JSON
  output places `agent_guidance` ahead of `findings`.
- Empty-field self-explanation — `agent_guidance_reason`,
  `related_files_reason`, and `likely_tests_reason` distinguish "we
  searched and found nothing" from "we didn't search".
- `_test.ts` / `_spec.ts` likely-test discovery.

All additions are additive and backwards-compatible. `schema_version`
stays at `"0.1.0"`. Live on npm. Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.4.0.md

## Detectors shipped in v0.6.0

Eighteen new detector types, grouped into four categories. All emit
the standard `Finding` shape; cross-file findings populate
`related_files`. Phrasing on the new detectors stays hedged ("appears
to" / "may") — they are signal, not assertions of semantic truth.

Architecture / dependency graph:
- `layer_violation` (charge: Layer Border Crossing) — an import that
  crosses a forbidden boundary defined by `architecture.layers` +
  `architecture.rules` in `crimes.config.json`.
- `circular_dependency` (charge: Tangled Imports) — strongly-connected
  components in the import graph (≥ 2 files).
- `deep_import` (charge: Deep Import Abuse) — imports reaching
  more than `n` segments deep into another package's source tree.
- `high_fan_in_fan_out` (charge: Crowded Module) — files with unusually
  high import fan-in and/or fan-out.

Information-architecture completion:
- `orphaned_destination` (charge: Orphaned Destination) — routes
  declared in nav / IA index without a resolving source / route file.
- `parallel_destination` (charge: Parallel Destination) — two nav-like
  surfaces declaring different routes for the same canonical destination.
- `permission_ia_drift` (charge: Permission IA Drift) — same role /
  permission identifier categorised differently across surfaces.
- `action_label_drift` (charge: Action Label Drift) — same domain
  action labelled differently across surfaces.
- `command_drift_docs_code_drift` (charge: Command Docs / Code Drift) —
  docs reference a `bin` subcommand the CLI no longer implements.

Frontend / UI agent-risk:
- `design_token_escape` (charge: Design Token Escape) — hard-coded
  colours / spacings / font sizes where the repo has a design-token
  system.
- `accessible_interaction_risk` (charge: Hidden Interaction) — JSX
  element handles pointer events but has no accessible label.
- `duplicate_component_shape` (charge: Duplicate Component Shape) —
  two or more components with AST-hash-equivalent JSX shape.
- `responsive_fragility` (charge: Responsive Fragility) — component
  mixing many breakpoint-specific utilities or hard-pixel widths.
- `copy_ia_drift` (charge: Copy / IA Drift, frontend variant) — nav
  label and breadcrumb / page-title disagree on the same destination.
- `visual_regression_review_hint` (charge: Visual Regression Review
  Hint) — a changed line touches JSX in a way the heuristics suspect
  will alter the rendered output.

Duplication:
- `exact_duplicate_block` (charge: Exact Duplicate Block) — function
  bodies with identical AST hashes across two or more files.
- `near_duplicate_block` (charge: Near-Duplicate Block) — function
  bodies with high-similarity AST-hash bags but small deltas.
- `duplicated_role_status_plan_check` (charge: Duplicated Policy
  Logic) — same domain concept (role / status / plan) checked in
  multiple files with different shapes.

Per-finding scores (M2 completion in v0.6.0):
- `scores.blast_radius` — normalised transitive-importer count.
- `scores.churn` — normalised commits-in-window count.
- `scores.test_gap` — three-tier signal from filesystem + import-graph
  test discovery.
- Unified `agent_risk` formula combines all five inputs. Documented in
  `docs/scoring.md`.

Additional v0.6.0 shape: `cli_command_registrar` for `large_function`
— Commander-style `register*Command(program)` wrappers and their
anonymous `.action(...)` callbacks no longer false-positive at the
domain threshold.

Full Starlight-rendered docs at https://crimes.sh/docs/.

## Shipped in v0.5.0 — suppressions, config, and explainability

v0.5.0 is the product-surface release: six new commands and a real
config story without any new detectors.

- `crimes init [--force]` — bootstrap `crimes.config.json` with
  sensible defaults and inline pointers at the new knobs. Refuses to
  overwrite without `--force`. Non-interactive on purpose.
- `crimes ignore <id-or-fingerprint> --reason "…"` — suppress one
  specific finding. Writes `.crimes/suppressions.json`,
  fingerprint-keyed, with a required non-empty `reason`. The file is
  intended to be committed and reviewed in PRs.
- `crimes unignore <fingerprint>` — remove a suppression entry by
  stable fingerprint. Symmetric to `crimes ignore`; supports
  `--dry-run` and `--file <path>`. Empty `suppressions: []` is left
  in place so the file frame is visible to reviewers.
- `crimes audit-suppressions [--format human|json]` — list every
  entry sorted oldest first, with `age_days` and per-entry concerns
  (`stale` > 180 days, `short_reason` < 16 chars, `vague_reason` for
  deferral keywords like `tmp` / `todo` / `wip` / `too noisy`).
  Emits `report_type: "audit_suppressions"`.
- `crimes explain <id-or-fingerprint> [--from <scan.json>]` —
  deterministic long-form rationale for one finding. Prints the
  detector description, a one-paragraph `why_it_matters`, the
  finding's evidence, suggested actions, related files, and the
  verbatim `crimes ignore` command line. No LLM, no network.
- `crimes diff --fail-on new-high | new-medium` — completes the M4
  CI-gate trio. Exits `1` when any new finding meets the threshold.
- `--show-suppressed` on `scan`, `context`, `baseline check`, `diff`,
  `verdict` — re-surfaces matched findings annotated with
  `suppressed: true` and `suppression_reason`.

Config additions (all optional, all back-compat):

- `thresholds.largeFunction.<shape>` — per-shape overrides for
  `domain` / `route_handler` / `react_component` / `page_export` /
  `test_callback` / `unknown`.
- `detectors.enable` / `detectors.disable` — allowlist / blocklist on
  detector ids. Unknown ids exit `2`.
- `ia.aliasGroups` — additive to the built-in `DEFAULT_ALIAS_GROUPS`.
- `suppressions.path` — override the suppressions file location.
- `architecture.layers` / `architecture.rules` — reserved
  placeholder, schema-validated but not consumed in v0.5.0.

Schema additions (all optional / additive — **no `schema_version`
bump**):

- `Finding.suppressed` / `Finding.suppression_reason` (only present
  with `--show-suppressed`).
- `*Report.suppressed_count` on `ScanReport`, `ContextReport`,
  `BaselineCheckReport`, `DiffReport`, `VerdictReport`.
- `DiffReport.fail_on` / `DiffReport.failed` (only present with
  `--fail-on`).
- New report types: `ExplainReport` (`report_type: "explain"`),
  on-disk `Suppressions` (`report_type: "suppressions"`), and
  `AuditSuppressionsReport` (`report_type: "audit_suppressions"`).

Suppressions are applied **before** every `--fail-on` evaluation. A
suppressed finding never trips a gate, regardless of severity or
which gating command is used.

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.5.0.md

## 0.9.2 — emoji severity glyphs + ortomate move (current release)

UX-only patch. No new detectors, no schema change.

- Severity glyphs in the human report. 🚨 high · ⚠️ medium · 🔎 low
  prefix every finding's title line and the severity heading.
  ✅ / ❌ on the `--fail-on` gate line; ✨ on the "no crimes
  detected" empty state. Suppressed when stdout isn't a TTY, when
  `NO_COLOR` is set, or when `--no-color` is passed — JSON output,
  CI logs, and piped invocations stay emoji-free.
- Repository moved from `andrewfantastic/crimes` to
  `ortomate/crimes`. `repository.url` and `bugs.url` in the
  published package now point at the new GitHub org; every
  documentation deep link follows. npm Trusted Publisher config
  moved to the new org in the same cut-over.

Schema unchanged (`schema_version: "0.1.0"`).

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.9.2.md

## 0.9.1 — visible welcome banner on bare `crimes`

UX-only patch. No new detectors, no schema change.

- Bare `crimes` (no subcommand) prints a short welcome banner
  with the version, three first-step commands
  (`crimes init --agents`, `crimes init`, `crimes --help`), and
  a docs link — instead of Commander's long help dump.
  `crimes --help` still renders the full usage output;
  `crimes --version` still prints just the version.
- Post-install message expanded to include the version and the
  same three commands. The script still runs, but npm 7+
  swallows post-install stdout / stderr by default, so the
  bare-`crimes` banner is the reliable surface. Users who want
  to see post-install output need
  `npm install -g --foreground-scripts crimes` or
  `foreground-scripts=true` in `~/.npmrc`.

Schema unchanged (`schema_version: "0.1.0"`).

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.9.1.md

## 0.9.0 — Codex agent discovery + petty crime

Minor bump. One new detector and an expanded agent surface; the
`Finding` wire format is unchanged.

- Codex agent discovery is first-class. `crimes init --agents`
  writes both `.claude/skills/crimes/SKILL.md` and
  `.agents/skills/crimes/SKILL.md`; a new `--codex-skill` flag
  writes only the Codex skill. The `missing_agent_context`
  detector recognises `.agents/skills/*/SKILL.md` as a satisfying
  signal — so a repo with a Codex skill but no `AGENTS.md` /
  `CLAUDE.md` no longer fires.
- New detector `finder_duplicate_filename` (petty). Flags macOS
  Finder / iCloud conflict-copy filenames like `Button 2.tsx`
  that slip into repos as accidental duplicates. Filename-only;
  requires the Finder-style space before the number, so
  `Page2.tsx` and `v2.ts` are not flagged. Medium severity, 0.90
  confidence.
- `crimes explain` rewrite. Output broken into named section
  helpers; new "Likely remedies" block synthesised from the
  finding's `suggested_actions` plus two generic next-steps. The
  `ExplainReport` JSON gains a new `likely_remedies: string[]`
  field.
- `npm install -g crimes` now prints a one-line nudge to run
  `crimes init --agents`. Suppressed in CI and behind
  `CRIMES_DISABLE_POSTINSTALL=1`.

Schema unchanged (`schema_version: "0.1.0"`).

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.9.0.md

## 0.8.1 — calibration patch

Patch on top of 0.8.0. No new detectors, no schema change.

- `boolean_naming_drift` built-in allowlist expanded with eight
  idiomatic state names (`loaded`, `found`, `settled`, `overflow`,
  `typeonly`, `interpolated`, `limited`, `existed`). Pure default-
  tuning; project configs unaffected.
- `scan-assets.ts` refactored into four named helpers
  (`discoverAssetFiles`, `groupDetectorsByExtension`,
  `runDetectorsForAssetFile`, `buildAssetContext`). Behaviour
  byte-identical.
- Crimes-monorepo dogfood: `crimes.config.json` excludes
  `evals/fixtures/**` and `examples/messy-ts-app/**` from the
  asset pass. No effect on downstream users.

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.8.1.md

## 0.8.0 — extended lens

Thirteen new detectors plus one config feature. Each emits the
existing `Finding` shape — no schema bump.

- `detectors.options.<id>` — per-detector exemption values
  (allowlists, threshold tuning). Sits between `detectors.disable`
  (kills the detector everywhere) and `crimes ignore` (suppresses
  one specific finding). Each detector registers its own zod schema;
  typos surface at config-load time.

Date / time family (5 detectors):
- `timezone_unsafe_parse` — `new Date("…")` with no `Z` / `±HH:MM` offset.
- `mixed_utc_local_methods` — UTC + local Date methods on the same receiver.
- `locale_drift` — `.toLocaleDateString()` / etc. with no locale arg.
- `dst_naive_arithmetic` — `+ 86400000` and folded `24 * 60 * 60 * 1000`.
- `date_string_concat` — `"…" + d.dateMethod()` hand-rolled date strings.

Naming-tier family (2 detectors):
- `boolean_naming_drift` — booleans without `is`/`has`/`should`/`can`/…
  prefix (built-in React-state allowlist; user extension via
  `detectors.options.boolean_naming_drift.allowedNames`).
- `singular_plural_type_mismatch` — `users: User`, `invoice: Invoice[]`
  shape disagreement between name and type annotation.

Hot-path / portability family (3 detectors):
- `sync_io_in_hotpath` — `readFileSync` / `execSync` / etc. inside
  route handlers, page exports, React components, or domain
  functions (test / CLI registrar ancestors suppress).
- `hardcoded_local_path` — `/Users/<name>/…`, `/home/<name>/…`,
  Windows `C:\Users\<name>\…` baked into non-test source.
- `hardcoded_localhost` — `localhost:NNNN`, `127.0.0.1:NNNN`,
  `0.0.0.0:NNNN`, `[::1]:NNNN` outside config-style basenames.

Asset family (3 detectors) — first non-source detectors:
- `oversized_raster` — file size against `thresholds.assetWeight.{low,medium,high}Kb`
  (defaults 200 / 500 / 1000 KB).
- `raster_should_be_vector` — PNG / JPEG / GIF whose width AND height
  both fit ≤ 64 px (configurable `iconSizeMax`).
- `svg_with_embedded_raster` — SVGs containing `<image href="data:image/*;base64,…">`.

Asset detectors run a separate second-pass walk over
`**/*.{png,jpg,jpeg,gif,webp,avif,svg}`. Configured via
`assets.include` / `assets.exclude`.

Release notes:
https://github.com/ortomate/crimes/blob/main/docs/releases/v0.8.0.md

## Not shipped yet — do not invoke or document as available

- `crimes ask` / LLM-assisted features — v1+.
- Homebrew tap + standalone binaries — deferred.
- Suppression expiry / owner (`expires_at`, `owner`) — designed but deferred.
- `date_equality_misuse` — deferred to 0.9.0 with type-info work.
- `exported_stop_word_name` — researched and dropped (exports-only
  prototype found zero hits across 500+ files).
- `dead_export` agent-risk variant (edit-distance match against
  live exports) — deferred to 0.9.0; needs eval-run evidence first.
- WebP / AVIF coverage on `raster_should_be_vector` — v1 supports
  PNG / JPEG / GIF only (the in-tree header parser).
